title: Fire on the Velvet Horizon - Take a look at the best Monster Manual ever written...
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qzy12XbaJQ4

Daniel (@dungeondive) makes the case that Fire on the Velvet Horizon, by Patrick Stuart and Scrap Princess, is the finest monster manual ever written — and as a postscript to his Kingdom Death run, it shares that same gift for monsters that read like fever dreams. Crucially, it's 100% system-agnostic: no hit dice, no stats, nothing. Just roughly 75 creatures rendered in exquisite, verbose, purple prose that he experiences less as a game supplement than as a work of weird fiction.

He reads generously from it — the smoke-and-candle-wax askefits, the flute-voiced crypto spider that trades caught thoughts for a warm pocket, the madness-fed catastrophe tree, the bile-yellow Tasty Newt Boys, the dream-violent Imperator Ape — to show how the entries plant seeds of creativity for any worldbuilder, GM or writer. Scrap Princess's abstract, scribbly illustrations only *suggest* their subjects, which he loves: the gaps let the reader's imagination outdo any literal drawing, while the page layouts themselves echo each creature, House of Leaves-style.

Print-on-demand and only around twenty dollars, it earns his highest shelf. He places Stuart and Scrap Princess alongside Michael Cisco, Thomas Ligotti and J.M. McDermott — authors pushing the boundaries of weird fiction — and promises much more of their work on the channel.

----

What's the most imaginative, least "stat-block" monster book you've encountered — one you'd read for pleasure, not just reference?
